Motion To Compel Further Production Of Documents From Defendant Acton, Inc.
Off Calendar Aug-03-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Brittany Rogers, Esq. (415.544.0115; brittany@righthandlaw.net) of The Law Offices of Scott Righthand for Plaintiff.
Barbara L. Miller, Esq. (925.222.3414; BMiller@wshblaw.com) of Wood Smith Henning Berman for Defendant (audio only).

THE PARTIES STIPULATE TO THE MATTER BEING DECIDED BY THE JUDGE PRO TEM. THE PARTIES AND THE JUDGE PRO TEM APPEARING REMOTELY AUTHORIZE THE CLERK TO SIGN THE FORMAL STIPULATION FORM ON THEIR BEHALF.

ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFFS ELLIOTT CLARK and CARRIE CLARK's Motion To Compel Further Production Of Documents From Defendant Acton, Inc.:

Plaintiffs' Motion To Compel Further Production Of Documents From Defendant Acton, Inc. is GRANTED. All objections are overruled. Defendant Acton, Inc. first argues that the responsive documents are not in its possession, custody or control. (Opp. section IIA). However, Defendant also states that "[t]he only way for Defendant to replicate the complete ticketing information is to open each ticket individually and screenshot each section of all one hundred and ninety tickets." (Citing Cai Dep. 151:4-152:4). Therefore, it appears that the responsive documents are in Defendant's possession and control. Defendant next argues that the task of replicating the ticketing information is unduly burdensome. (Opp., sections IIB, C). However, this objection does not appear in Defendant's responses (See SS, p. 2-12) and was therefore waived. Defendant shall serve verified, code-compliant, amended responses omitting the objections and responsive documents within two weeks of notice of this order. Plaintiffs' request for sanctions against Defendant is GRANTED. Sanctions in the amount of $4,000 are awarded in favor of Plaintiffs and against Defendant Acton, Inc. Sanctions are due within three weeks of notice of this order.

Moving party to submit proposed order in compliance with CRC 3.1312 to JPT by 5pm with copy to all counsel via email at peter.catalanotti@wilsonelser.com.

Judge Pro Tem: Peter Catalanotti, Clerk: S. Le, Not reported. =(302/JPT)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jul-02-2020 Continued To Aug-26-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE
Off Calendar Jun-18-2020 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. BRITTANY ROGERS, Esq. of the Law Offices of Scott Righthand (Tel: 415-544-0115) appearing via CourtCall for plaintiff Elliott Clark;

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT ACTON, INC.'S MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE.

Matter not contested or argued.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling: Defendant Acton Inc.'s ("Acton") motion to change venue to Alameda County is denied. A court determines venue based upon the complaint on file and San Francisco venue is proper as the evidence shows that co-defendant Indiegogo, Inc. has its principal place of business in San Francisco. (Rogers Dec., Ex. D; Ferguson v. Koerber (1924) 69 Cal.App. 47, 49; Brown v. Superior Court (1984) 37 Cal.3d 477, 482 ["Venue is determined based on the complaint on file at the time the motion to change venue is made."].) The fact that plaintiff dismissed Indiegogo, Inc. does not change the analysis. Subsequent amendments to the complaint or dismissals do not make a proper choice of venue suddenly improper. (See Armstrong Petroleum Corp. v. Superior Court (1981) 114 Cal.App.3d 732, 738.)

Nevertheless, Acton correctly argues that the court can look behind the complaint to see if a defendant was improperly or fraudulently joined to the action to manufacture venue. (See Lachman Co. v. Central California Berry Growers' Assn. (1922) 58 Cal.App 748.) "In determining the question whether the resident defendant has been joined solely for the purpose of having the action tried in the county of his residence 'the real issue for determination by the trial court was whether plaintiff, in joining the resident defendant as a party, had reasonable grounds for the belief in good faith that plaintiff had a cause of action against the resident defendant.'" (Gottesfeld v. Richmaid Ice Cream Co. (1953) 115 Cal.App.2d 854, 856.)

Here, Acton has not shown that there was no basis to name Indiegogo. Plaintiff had a good faith basis for naming Indiegogo as a defendant in this products liability action. Plaintiff purchased the skateboard from Indiegogo's website and it was reasonable to assume that Indiegogo could be liable as a distributor or. for some other reason for being in the chain of commerce. (Rogers Dec., Ex. E.) The contours of crowdfunding website liability are far from clear.

Acton's motion to change venue based on the convenience of witnesses and ends of justice is denied without prejudice as premature. The court cannot rule on it because Acton's answer is not on file. (See Buran Equipment Co., Inc. v. Superior Court (1987) 190 Cal.App.3d 1662, 1665.)

The court denies plaintiff's request for sanctions.

Plaintiff's counsel shall email a proposed order to contestdept302tr@sftc.org.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/EPS)
